---
title: "One Tool to Rule Them All"
subtitle: "Quarto for Documentation, Presentations, Manuscripts and Outreach"
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    incremental: false
---

## Why Quarto?

- Research produces many related outputs: notes, manuscripts, slides, websites, dashboards
- These are usually made with disconnected tools and manual formatting
- Quarto: write once in plain text, render to HTML, PDF, DOCX, slides, and more
- Focus on content, not tool juggling

## Workshop Roadmap

1. **Foundations**: Quarto, Markdown, YAML, installation
2. **Use cases**: research diary, manuscripts, presentations, websites, dashboards
3. Each section has a guided exercise with an academic scenario

# Foundations

## What Is Quarto?

- Open-source scientific publishing system from Posit
- The multi-language, multi-format successor to R Markdown
- Three ingredients in one `.qmd` file:
  - **YAML** for metadata and configuration
  - **Markdown** for written content
  - **Code chunks** (R, Python, Julia, OJS) for computation

## Key Strengths

- **Plain text**: every change is a visible, trackable line (version control friendly)
- **One source, many outputs**: HTML, PDF, DOCX, slides from the same file
- **Executable code**: results are computed at render time, not pasted in
- Familiar if you know LaTeX, but simpler markup and more output formats

## Markdown in 30 Seconds

- Lightweight syntax for structure: headings, lists, links, images, emphasis
- `# Title`, `## Section`, `**bold**`, `*italic*`, `[link](url)`
- Quarto adds academic extras: citations, cross-references, callouts, figures with captions
- Mindset: describe *what* things are, let the renderer handle *how* they look

## YAML: Configuring Your Document

- The block between `---` fences at the top of a file
- Controls title, authors, date, output format, and options

```yaml
---
title: "My Analysis"
author: "Jane Doe"
format: pdf
---
```

- Key : value pairs; indentation matters
- Same content, different YAML = different publication

## Installing Quarto

- One install covers every output format
- You may already have it (bundled with RStudio and Positron)
- Recommended: Quarto CLI + an IDE (RStudio, VS Code, or Positron)
- PDF output needs a LaTeX distribution: `quarto install tinytex`
- Self-check: run `quarto check` in your terminal

# Use Cases

## Research Diary

- Capture what you tried, what worked, and *why you decided*
- Markdown headings separate entries by date or experiment
- Code chunks put data checks and figures right beside your notes
- Render to HTML for browsing, PDF for archiving
- Durable, shareable, searchable plain text

## Manuscripts

- Narrative, citations, figures, and tables in one reproducible workflow
- HTML/DOCX for collaborators, PDF/LaTeX for journal submission
- Journal templates available (quarto-journals)
- Inline code pulls statistics straight from your data: no stale numbers
- Bonus: papers written in Quarto combine easily into a cumulative thesis

## Presentations

- reveal.js slides: web-native, viewable in any browser
- Reuse figures, tables, and citations from your other `.qmd` files
- Embed interactive content: live code, diagrams, iframes
- Export to PowerPoint or PDF when required
- 11 built-in themes, or your own CSS branding
- Caveat: not the tool for design-heavy, graphics-first slides

## Websites

- Full websites from the same Markdown + YAML you already know
- Free hosting via GitHub Pages or Netlify
- `_quarto.yml` manages navigation, themes, and shared settings
- `index.qmd` is the front page
- Great for lab pages, project sites, teaching materials, personal profiles

## Dashboards

- `format: dashboard` turns a `.qmd` into a visual overview page
- Headings define rows; cards hold value boxes, charts, and tables
- Interactive in the browser with no server: Plotly charts, sortable tables, tabsets
- Limit: real reactivity (recomputing on input) needs Shiny and a server

# Wrap-Up

## One System, Many Outputs

- Same skills everywhere: Markdown + YAML + optional code
- Diary -> manuscript -> slides -> website -> dashboard, from one project
- Start with the foundations, then pick the use case you need today
- Exercises and reference pages: this workshop website

## Before You Start

- Install Quarto >= 1.5
- Run `quarto check` to verify your setup
- Pick an editor: RStudio, VS Code, or Positron
- Questions? Ask us, or see quarto.org
